highlightedCommentsActivityCounts: ({'text': 'Thanks for having me, Kevin. I had a lot of fun chatting about the holy grail of computer science.', 'totalReactionCount': 29, 'likeCount': 25, 'appreciationCount': 1, 'InterestCount': 1, 'praiseCount': 2},)
text: Over the last few months, I’ve had the opportunity to meet with Bill Gates on a few occasions to talk about this unique moment within the history of computing and what the current AI revolution means for the future. Bill has had a front row view as we’ve moved through the PC revolution and the Internet revolution, and it was an honor to have him join me on #BehindTheTech. During our conversation, we reflected on our recent talks and discussed the latest in AI research, including the release of GPT-4, how AI is evolving his philanthropic work, how past technology revolutions have led us to where we are today, his love of reading, and so much more. Take a listen (https://lnkd.in/g5fgAjXB) or watch on YouTube (https://lnkd.in/gpTCd3bS). I am excited to hear what you all think!

text:
We had a special visitor at Covariant headquarters last week…Bill Gates!

Bill challenged the Covariant Brain — our AI Robotics platform — by arranging polybagged apparel items chaotically into one bin, and tiny cylindrical eyeliner pencils into another. 

Not to be thwarted, the Covariant Brain quickly analyzed the totes, found the optimal grasp points, and directed the robots to pick all the items successfully, with accuracy and ease.

Our robots can handle virtually any item you – or Bill Gates – throw at them. Thanks again to Bill for stopping by to experience the future of AI and robotic automation.

text:
Bill Gates zeigt, wie die Bekämpfung des Klimawandels funktionieren kann! Lösungsansätze über alle Sektoren werden diskutiert: Transport, Landwirtschaft, Energie, Zement, Stahl. Ein „can do approach“ - wie man es aus Amerika erwarten kann. In vielen seiner Einschätzungen stimme ich mit ihm überein. Wir schöpfen die technischen Möglichkeiten noch lange nicht aus - und Klimaschutz muss nicht immer Verzicht bedeuten.
Ein wichtiger Vorschlag von Bill Gates, entscheidender Hebel für Effizienz beim Klimaschutz: ein sektorenübergreifender #CO2-Preis - eine Forderung die heute viele Wissenschaftler und verantwortungsbewusste Politiker teilen.

Gates sieht im Klimaschutz auch neue Geschäftsmöglichkeiten: Investitionen in Innovationen zum Klimaschutz - er engagiert sich mit eigenem Vermögen in zahlreichen Klimaschutz Startups.
Auch zahlreiche Anknüpfungspunkte zu Volkswagen AG Projekten wie Energiespeicherung, Carbon-Capturing, Skalierung verschiedener Technologien sind erkennbar.

Lesenswert, motivierend, #Klimaschutz ist eine Chance - auch wirtschaftlich - für Innovatoren.

text: Bill Gates, you thoroughly understand the CO2 logic of the mobility sector. You know what we have to do and you could and should advise governments how to regulate and what to promote: As you say, it’s not too complicated – electric vehicles wherever possible:all passenger cars,most of the delivery vans,most of public transportation,carbage collection. Long haul on biofuels,2nd or 3rd generation produced out of waste or residuals - like we do at Scania Group. And you rightly do not talk about hydrogen fuel cells for the transport sector with their low efficiency and the 3 times higher energy demand compared to EVs. Synthetic fuels which are also highly energy intensive to produce will - even fully scaled up - remain very expensive but will be needed in transportation which can’t be electrified like planes.In my personal view,would be better to go ahead with motor racing:F1 becoming CO2 neutral using synthetic fuels is much more excitement,fun,racing experience,tech-competition than Formula E driving a few laps in city centers in gaming mode.All of this only makes sense if we get carbon-free electricity soon. It’s possible. But Germany’s target date to exit coal power only by 2038 is for a rich,tech-leading country by far not ambitious enough.Well thought,well written, Bill Gates!

text: On my first day as CEO at the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, I'm reflecting on the huge responsibility that comes with this job. Knowing that we at the foundation can't achieve our ambitious goals alone, I am deeply committed to building our community of partners and to ensuring that our culture at the foundation enables the impact we aspire to have. Building a community is easy to say, but I know that it will take a lot of work. So I've set myself a goal to take the extra time to connect with colleagues and partners, to listen to their experiences and ask them, and myself, the tough questions. I am especially excited to spend my first week as CEO in South Africa, where I was raised and where exposure to apartheid first instilled in me a desire to use the advantages afforded to me to help tackle injustice and inequality. I can't think of a better place to start this next chapter in my career (and I'm pretty happy that it gives me a chance to see my parents as well).

text:
Hey folks, We have a pretty exciting guest on the Next Big Idea podcast this week — Bill Gates.

The topic? Artificial Intelligence, and how it will change our world. 

Here's a small sampling of the points Bill makes in this conversation:

- Superhuman AI is coming. 
- There is no clear way to slow it down. 
- The technology available today is *already* a game changer. 
- We will all soon have intelligent agents that function as "executive assistants, mental therapists, and friends."
- AI has the potential to help us solve the most intractable global problems, it has the potential to help us all get along better, and it's likely to deliver us to a world of superabundance. But it also poses "novel" threats, given the pace of it's acceleration and the lack of "clear upper bound" of it's capability. 

Listen on Apple or Spotify ... I look forward to discussing with you in the comments below!

text:
I'm thrilled to embark on a new chapter as the Africa Director at the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation. 

For 12 years, I’ve been privileged to contribute to health and development initiatives, working alongside our inspiring partners to tackle the world’s most pressing development challenges.

Now, I’m honored to take on a new challenge leading our remarkable teams in Africa. I’m committed to finding innovative solutions to improve health outcomes, promote gender equality and financial inclusion, and build climate resilience and sustainable development.

text:
I'm so happy that after a three-year labor of love this is book is finally published. It argues that the combination of new findings in psychology and the incredible connectedness of the modern world create a new way to think about generosity. One that just might help turn the tide on today's mean, cynical, angry, divided culture.  Seems daunting... Maybe crazy...  But if kindness can go viral, all bets are off.  

I truly believe that any forward-leaning company can benefit from brainstorming its own generosity strategy.  And yes, I use the word strategy very deliberately. 

I really hope some of you will get a chance to read this. And I would be fascinated to hear what you make of it.  You can find links to the seller of your choice at https://lnkd.in/edg6JzZM  
(also a link to our delightful new generosity AI!)

P.S. All proceeds from the book go to support the mission of TED, the nonprofit where I've been joyfully volunteering these past 20 years.

text:
This shook me - in the best possible way. 

The wonderful behavioural scientists and psychologists at BEworks just launched a report here at #cop28 on personality traits, mindsets and climate change. 

One discovery they made - a shockingly strong correlation between creativity  and climate optimism! Basically, the more creative your personality then the more likely you are to see solutions and answers to the climate crisis.

I guess I shouldn’t be surprised. For my book The Solutionists I found much higher levels of climate optimism amoungst folks actually working on answers. But it’s stark to see the psychological correlation laid out like this. Inventiveness, lateral thinking, imagination - creative traits that can see a way through the fuggy mess we’re in.

Futerra has been bringing creativity and storytelling here to #cop28uae - but after the dust settles we’ll continue bringing climate to the entertainment industry, social media and the culture sector. Because we must recruit the creatives into climate action. If we want a better future, we need to imagine it first! 

On second thoughts, maybe I’m not that shocked by this research after all 😉

The full report is here: https://lnkd.in/gDVd5aqF

That’s one of the reasons I love commencement ceremonies so much—even though I never had one of my own: Because they mark the start of a new stage in life for everyone involved, one with more possibility and potential than before. And seeing the pride on the faces of students and families is always a highlight. But I’m particularly excited to address the 2023 graduates of Northern Arizona University's College of Engineering, Informatics, and Applied Sciences and College of the Environment, Forestry, and Natural Sciences because the school has become a national leader in addressing one of the biggest challenges facing higher education today: the value students receive from their college degrees.

text: Over the last few months, I’ve had the opportunity to meet with Bill Gates on a few occasions to talk about this unique moment within the history of computing and what the current AI revolution means for the future. Bill has had a front row view as we’ve moved through the PC revolution and the Internet revolution, and it was an honor to have him join me on #BehindTheTech. During our conversation, we reflected on our recent talks and discussed the latest in AI research, including the release of GPT-4, how AI is evolving his philanthropic work, how past technology revolutions have led us to where we are today, his love of reading, and so much more. Take a listen (https://lnkd.in/g5fgAjXB) or watch on YouTube (https://lnkd.in/gpTCd3bS). I am excited to hear what you all think!

text:
Bill Gates zeigt, wie die Bekämpfung des Klimawandels funktionieren kann! Lösungsansätze über alle Sektoren werden diskutiert: Transport, Landwirtschaft, Energie, Zement, Stahl. Ein „can do approach“ - wie man es aus Amerika erwarten kann. In vielen seiner Einschätzungen stimme ich mit ihm überein. Wir schöpfen die technischen Möglichkeiten noch lange nicht aus - und Klimaschutz muss nicht immer Verzicht bedeuten.
Ein wichtiger Vorschlag von Bill Gates, entscheidender Hebel für Effizienz beim Klimaschutz: ein sektorenübergreifender #CO2-Preis - eine Forderung die heute viele Wissenschaftler und verantwortungsbewusste Politiker teilen.

Gates sieht im Klimaschutz auch neue Geschäftsmöglichkeiten: Investitionen in Innovationen zum Klimaschutz - er engagiert sich mit eigenem Vermögen in zahlreichen Klimaschutz Startups.
Auch zahlreiche Anknüpfungspunkte zu Volkswagen AG Projekten wie Energiespeicherung, Carbon-Capturing, Skalierung verschiedener Technologien sind erkennbar.

Lesenswert, motivierend, #Klimaschutz ist eine Chance - auch wirtschaftlich - für Innovatoren.

text: Bill Gates, you thoroughly understand the CO2 logic of the mobility sector. You know what we have to do and you could and should advise governments how to regulate and what to promote: As you say, it’s not too complicated – electric vehicles wherever possible:all passenger cars,most of the delivery vans,most of public transportation,carbage collection. Long haul on biofuels,2nd or 3rd generation produced out of waste or residuals - like we do at Scania Group. And you rightly do not talk about hydrogen fuel cells for the transport sector with their low efficiency and the 3 times higher energy demand compared to EVs. Synthetic fuels which are also highly energy intensive to produce will - even fully scaled up - remain very expensive but will be needed in transportation which can’t be electrified like planes.In my personal view,would be better to go ahead with motor racing:F1 becoming CO2 neutral using synthetic fuels is much more excitement,fun,racing experience,tech-competition than Formula E driving a few laps in city centers in gaming mode.All of this only makes sense if we get carbon-free electricity soon. text: On my first day as CEO at the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, I'm reflecting on the huge responsibility that comes with this job. Knowing that we at the foundation can't achieve our ambitious goals alone, I am deeply committed to building our community of partners and to ensuring that our culture at the foundation enables the impact we aspire to have. Building a community is easy to say, but I know that it will take a lot of work. So I've set myself a goal to take the extra time to connect with colleagues and partners, to listen to their experiences and ask them, and myself, the tough questions. I am especially excited to spend my first week as CEO in South Africa, where I was raised and where exposure to apartheid first instilled in me a desire to use the advantages afforded to me to help tackle injustice and inequality. I can't think of a better place to start this next chapter in my career (and I'm pretty happy that it gives me a chance to see my parents as well).

text:
Hey folks, We have a pretty exciting guest on the Next Big Idea podcast this week — Bill Gates.

The topic? Artificial Intelligence, and how it will change our world. 

Here's a small sampling of the points Bill makes in this conversation:

- Superhuman AI is coming. 
- There is no clear way to slow it down. 
- The technology available today is *already* a game changer. 
- We will all soon have intelligent agents that function as "executive assistants, mental therapists, and friends."
- AI has the potential to help us solve the most intractable global problems, it has the potential to help us all get along better, and it's likely to deliver us to a world of superabundance. But it also poses "novel" threats, given the pace of it's acceleration and the lack of "clear upper bound" of it's capability. 

Listen on Apple or Spotify ... I look forward to discussing with you in the comments below!

text:
I'm thrilled to embark on a new chapter as the Africa Director at the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation. 

For 12 years, I’ve been privileged to contribute to health and development initiatives, working alongside our inspiring partners to tackle the world’s most pressing development challenges.

Now, I’m honored to take on a new challenge leading our remarkable teams in Africa. I’m committed to finding innovative solutions to improve health outcomes, promote gender equality and financial inclusion, and build climate resilience and sustainable development. 

Together with our partners and many governments we work with, we’ll champion innovative solutions, drive real progress, and advance the foundation’s mission of creating a world where everyone has the opportunity to live a healthy, productive life. 

#Leadership #Impact

text:
I'm so happy that after a three-year labor of love this is book is finally published. It argues that the combination of new findings in psychology and the incredible connectedness of the modern world create a new way to think about generosity. One that just might help turn the tide on today's mean, cynical, angry, divided culture.  Seems daunting... Maybe crazy...  But if kindness can go viral, all bets are off.  

I truly believe that any forward-leaning company can benefit from brainstorming its own generosity strategy.  And yes, I use the word strategy very deliberately. 

I really hope some of you will get a chance to read this. And I would be fascinated to hear what you make of it.  You can find links to the seller of your choice at https://lnkd.in/edg6JzZM  
(also a link to our delightful new generosity AI!)

P.S. All proceeds from the book go to support the mission of TED, the nonprofit where I've been joyfully volunteering these past 20 years.

text:
This shook me - in the best possible way. 

The wonderful behavioural scientists and psychologists at BEworks just launched a report here at #cop28 on personality traits, mindsets and climate change. 

One discovery they made - a shockingly strong correlation between creativity  and climate optimism! Basically, the more creative your personality then the more likely you are to see solutions and answers to the climate crisis.

I guess I shouldn’t be surprised. For my book The Solutionists I found much higher levels of climate optimism amoungst folks actually working on answers. But it’s stark to see the psychological correlation laid out like this. Inventiveness, lateral thinking, imagination - creative traits that can see a way through the fuggy mess we’re in.

Futerra has been bringing creativity and storytelling here to #cop28uae - but after the dust settles we’ll continue bringing climate to the entertainment industry, social media and the culture sector. Because we must recruit the creatives into climate action. If we want a better future, we need to imagine it first! 

On second thoughts, maybe I’m not that shocked by this research after all 😉

The full report is here: https://lnkd.in/gDVd5aqF

text: Education, opportunity, and economic mobility are inextricably linked. text: Over the last few months, I’ve had the opportunity to meet with Bill Gates on a few occasions to talk about this unique moment within the history of computing and what the current AI revolution means for the future. Bill has had a front row view as we’ve moved through the PC revolution and the Internet revolution, and it was an honor to have him join me on #BehindTheTech. During our conversation, we reflected on our recent talks and discussed the latest in AI research, including the release of GPT-4, how AI is evolving his philanthropic work, how past technology revolutions have led us to where we are today, his love of reading, and so much more. text:
We had a special visitor at Covariant headquarters last week…Bill Gates!

Bill challenged the Covariant Brain — our AI Robotics platform — by arranging polybagged apparel items chaotically into one bin, and tiny cylindrical eyeliner pencils into another.

Our robots can handle virtually any item you – or Bill Gates – throw at them. Thanks again to Bill for stopping by to experience the future of AI and robotic automation.

text:
Bill Gates zeigt, wie die Bekämpfung des Klimawandels funktionieren kann! Lösungsansätze über alle Sektoren werden diskutiert: Transport, Landwirtschaft, Energie, Zement, Stahl. Ein „can do approach“ - wie man es aus Amerika erwarten kann. In vielen seiner Einschätzungen stimme ich mit ihm überein. Wir schöpfen die technischen Möglichkeiten noch lange nicht aus - und Klimaschutz muss nicht immer Verzicht bedeuten.
Ein wichtiger Vorschlag von Bill Gates, entscheidender Hebel für Effizienz beim Klimaschutz: ein sektorenübergreifender #CO2-Preis - eine Forderung die heute viele Wissenschaftler und verantwortungsbewusste Politiker teilen.

Gates sieht im Klimaschutz auch neue Geschäftsmöglichkeiten: Investitionen in Innovationen zum Klimaschutz - er engagiert sich mit eigenem Vermögen in zahlreichen Klimaschutz Startups.
Auch zahlreiche Anknüpfungspunkte zu Volkswagen AG Projekten wie Energiespeicherung, Carbon-Capturing, Skalierung verschiedener Technologien sind erkennbar.

Lesenswert, motivierend, #Klimaschutz ist eine Chance - auch wirtschaftlich - für Innovatoren.

text: On my first day as CEO at the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, I'm reflecting on the huge responsibility that comes with this job. Knowing that we at the foundation can't achieve our ambitious goals alone, I am deeply committed to building our community of partners and to ensuring that our culture at the foundation enables the impact we aspire to have. Building a community is easy to say, but I know that it will take a lot of work. So I've set myself a goal to take the extra time to connect with colleagues and partners, to listen to their experiences and ask them, and myself, the tough questions. I am especially excited to spend my first week as CEO in South Africa, where I was raised and where exposure to apartheid first instilled in me a desire to use the advantages afforded to me to help tackle injustice and inequality. I can't think of a better place to start this next chapter in my career (and I'm pretty happy that it gives me a chance to see my parents as well).

text:
Hey folks, We have a pretty exciting guest on the Next Big Idea podcast this week — Bill Gates.

The topic? Artificial Intelligence, and how it will change our world. 

Here's a small sampling of the points Bill makes in this conversation:

- Superhuman AI is coming. 
- There is no clear way to slow it down. 
- The technology available today is *already* a game changer. 
- We will all soon have intelligent agents that function as "executive assistants, mental therapists, and friends."
- AI has the potential to help us solve the most intractable global problems, it has the potential to help us all get along better, and it's likely to deliver us to a world of superabundance. But it also poses "novel" threats, given the pace of it's acceleration and the lack of "clear upper bound" of it's capability. 

Listen on Apple or Spotify ... I look forward to discussing with you in the comments below!

text:
This shook me - in the best possible way. 

The wonderful behavioural scientists and psychologists at BEworks just launched a report here at #cop28 on personality traits, mindsets and climate change. 

One discovery they made - a shockingly strong correlation between creativity  and climate optimism! Basically, the more creative your personality then the more likely you are to see solutions and answers to the climate crisis.

I guess I shouldn’t be surprised. For my book The Solutionists I found much higher levels of climate optimism amoungst folks actually working on answers. But it’s stark to see the psychological correlation laid out like this. Inventiveness, lateral thinking, imagination - creative traits that can see a way through the fuggy mess we’re in.

Futerra has been bringing creativity and storytelling here to #cop28uae - but after the dust settles we’ll continue bringing climate to the entertainment industry, social media and the culture sector. Because we must recruit the creatives into climate action. If we want a better future, we need to imagine it first! 

On second thoughts, maybe I’m not that shocked by this research after all 😉

The full report is here: https://lnkd.in/gDVd5aqF

text: Education, opportunity, and economic mobility are inextricably linked. That’s one of the reasons I love commencement ceremonies so much—even though I never had one of my own: Because they mark the start of a new stage in life for everyone involved, one with more possibility and potential than before. And seeing the pride on the faces of students and families is always a highlight. text: Over the last few months, I’ve had the opportunity to meet with Bill Gates on a few occasions to talk about this unique moment within the history of computing and what the current AI revolution means for the future. text:
Bill Gates zeigt, wie die Bekämpfung des Klimawandels funktionieren kann! Lösungsansätze über alle Sektoren werden diskutiert: Transport, Landwirtschaft, Energie, Zement, Stahl. Ein „can do approach“ - wie man es aus Amerika erwarten kann. In vielen seiner Einschätzungen stimme ich mit ihm überein. Wir schöpfen die technischen Möglichkeiten noch lange nicht aus - und Klimaschutz muss nicht immer Verzicht bedeuten.
Ein wichtiger Vorschlag von Bill Gates, entscheidender Hebel für Effizienz beim Klimaschutz: ein sektorenübergreifender #CO2-Preis - eine Forderung die heute viele Wissenschaftler und verantwortungsbewusste Politiker teilen.

Gates sieht im Klimaschutz auch neue Geschäftsmöglichkeiten: Investitionen in Innovationen zum Klimaschutz - er engagiert sich mit eigenem Vermögen in zahlreichen Klimaschutz Startups.
Auch zahlreiche Anknüpfungspunkte zu Volkswagen AG Projekten wie Energiespeicherung, Carbon-Capturing, Skalierung verschiedener Technologien sind erkennbar.

Lesenswert, motivierend, #Klimaschutz ist eine Chance - auch wirtschaftlich - für Innovatoren.

text: Bill Gates, you thoroughly understand the CO2 logic of the mobility sector. You know what we have to do and you could and should advise governments how to regulate and what to promote: As you say, it’s not too complicated – electric vehicles wherever possible:all passenger cars,most of the delivery vans,most of public transportation,carbage collection. Long haul on biofuels,2nd or 3rd generation produced out of waste or residuals - like we do at Scania Group. And you rightly do not talk about hydrogen fuel cells for the transport sector with their low efficiency and the 3 times higher energy demand compared to EVs. Synthetic fuels which are also highly energy intensive to produce will - even fully scaled up - remain very expensive but will be needed in transportation which can’t be electrified like planes.In my personal view,would be better to go ahead with motor racing:F1 becoming CO2 neutral using synthetic fuels is much more excitement,fun,racing experience,tech-competition than Formula E driving a few laps in city centers in gaming mode.All of this only makes sense if we get carbon-free electricity soon. It’s possible. But Germany’s target date to exit coal power only by 2038 is for a rich,tech-leading country by far not ambitious enough.Well thought,well written, Bill Gates!

text: On my first day as CEO at the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, I'm reflecting on the huge responsibility that comes with this job. Knowing that we at the foundation can't achieve our ambitious goals alone, I am deeply committed to building our community of partners and to ensuring that our culture at the foundation enables the impact we aspire to have. Building a community is easy to say, but I know that it will take a lot of work. So I've set myself a goal to take the extra time to connect with colleagues and partners, to listen to their experiences and ask them, and myself, the tough questions. I am especially excited to spend my first week as CEO in South Africa, where I was raised and where exposure to apartheid first instilled in me a desire to use the advantages afforded to me to help tackle injustice and inequality. I can't think of a better place to start this next chapter in my career (and I'm pretty happy that it gives me a chance to see my parents as well).

text:
Hey folks, We have a pretty exciting guest on the Next Big Idea podcast this week — Bill Gates.

The topic? Artificial Intelligence, and how it will change our world. 

Here's a small sampling of the points Bill makes in this conversation:

- Superhuman AI is coming. 
- There is no clear way to slow it down. 
- The technology available today is *already* a game changer. 
- We will all soon have intelligent agents that function as "executive assistants, mental therapists, and friends."
- AI has the potential to help us solve the most intractable global problems, it has the potential to help us all get along better, and it's likely to deliver us to a world of superabundance. But it also poses "novel" threats, given the pace of it's acceleration and the lack of "clear upper bound" of it's capability. 

Listen on Apple or Spotify ... I look forward to discussing with you in the comments below!

text:
I'm thrilled to embark on a new chapter as the Africa Director at the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation. 

For 12 years, I’ve been privileged to contribute to health and development initiatives, working alongside our inspiring partners to tackle the world’s most pressing development challenges.

Now, I’m honored to take on a new challenge leading our remarkable teams in Africa. I’m committed to finding innovative solutions to improve health outcomes, promote gender equality and financial inclusion, and build climate resilience and sustainable development. 

Together with our partners and many governments we work with, we’ll champion innovative solutions, drive real progress, and advance the foundation’s mission of creating a world where everyone has the opportunity to live a healthy, productive life.

text:
I'm so happy that after a three-year labor of love this is book is finally published. It argues that the combination of new findings in psychology and the incredible connectedness of the modern world create a new way to think about generosity. One that just might help turn the tide on today's mean, cynical, angry, divided culture.  Seems daunting... Maybe crazy...  But if kindness can go viral, all bets are off.  

I truly believe that any forward-leaning company can benefit from brainstorming its own generosity strategy.  And yes, I use the word strategy very deliberately. 

I really hope some of you will get a chance to read this. And I would be fascinated to hear what you make of it.  You can find links to the seller of your choice at https://lnkd.in/edg6JzZM  
(also a link to our delightful new generosity AI!)

P.S. All proceeds from the book go to support the mission of TED, the nonprofit where I've been joyfully volunteering these past 20 years.

highlightedCommentsActivityCounts: ({'text': 'This is great.', 'totalReactionCount': 19, 'likeCount': 16, 'appreciationCount': 2, 'empathyCount': 1},)
text:
This shook me - in the best possible way. 

The wonderful behavioural scientists and psychologists at BEworks just launched a report here at #cop28 on personality traits, mindsets and climate change. 

One discovery they made - a shockingly strong correlation between creativity  and climate optimism! Basically, the more creative your personality then the more likely you are to see solutions and answers to the climate crisis.

I guess I shouldn’t be surprised. For my book The Solutionists I found much higher levels of climate optimism amoungst folks actually working on answers. But it’s stark to see the psychological correlation laid out like this. Inventiveness, lateral thinking, imagination - creative traits that can see a way through the fuggy mess we’re in.

Futerra has been bringing creativity and storytelling here to #cop28uae - but after the dust settles we’ll continue bringing climate to the entertainment industry, social media and the culture sector. Because we must recruit the creatives into climate action. If we want a better future, we need to imagine it first! 

On second thoughts, maybe I’m not that shocked by this research after all 😉

The full report is here: https://lnkd.in/gDVd5aqF

text: Over the last few months, I’ve had the opportunity to meet with Bill Gates on a few occasions to talk about this unique moment within the history of computing and what the current AI revolution means for the future. Bill has had a front row view as we’ve moved through the PC revolution and the Internet revolution, and it was an honor to have him join me on #BehindTheTech. During our conversation, we reflected on our recent talks and discussed the latest in AI research, including the release of GPT-4, how AI is evolving his philanthropic work, how past technology revolutions have led us to where we are today, his love of reading, and so much more. text:
Bill Gates zeigt, wie die Bekämpfung des Klimawandels funktionieren kann! Lösungsansätze über alle Sektoren werden diskutiert: Transport, Landwirtschaft, Energie, Zement, Stahl. Ein „can do approach“ - wie man es aus Amerika erwarten kann. In vielen seiner Einschätzungen stimme ich mit ihm überein. Wir schöpfen die technischen Möglichkeiten noch lange nicht aus - und Klimaschutz muss nicht immer Verzicht bedeuten.
Ein wichtiger Vorschlag von Bill Gates, entscheidender Hebel für Effizienz beim Klimaschutz: ein sektorenübergreifender #CO2-Preis - eine Forderung die heute viele Wissenschaftler und verantwortungsbewusste Politiker teilen.

Gates sieht im Klimaschutz auch neue Geschäftsmöglichkeiten: Investitionen in Innovationen zum Klimaschutz - er engagiert sich mit eigenem Vermögen in zahlreichen Klimaschutz Startups.
Auch zahlreiche Anknüpfungspunkte zu Volkswagen AG Projekten wie Energiespeicherung, Carbon-Capturing, Skalierung verschiedener Technologien sind erkennbar.

Lesenswert, motivierend, #Klimaschutz ist eine Chance - auch wirtschaftlich - für Innovatoren.

text: Bill Gates, you thoroughly understand the CO2 logic of the mobility sector. You know what we have to do and you could and should advise governments how to regulate and what to promote: As you say, it’s not too complicated – electric vehicles wherever possible:all passenger cars,most of the delivery vans,most of public transportation,carbage collection. Long haul on biofuels,2nd or 3rd generation produced out of waste or residuals - like we do at Scania Group. And you rightly do not talk about hydrogen fuel cells for the transport sector with their low efficiency and the 3 times higher energy demand compared to EVs. Synthetic fuels which are also highly energy intensive to produce will - even fully scaled up - remain very expensive but will be needed in transportation which can’t be electrified like planes.In my personal view,would be better to go ahead with motor racing:F1 becoming CO2 neutral using synthetic fuels is much more excitement,fun,racing experience,tech-competition than Formula E driving a few laps in city centers in gaming mode.All of this only makes sense if we get carbon-free electricity soon. It’s possible. But Germany’s target date to exit coal power only by 2038 is for a rich,tech-leading country by far not ambitious enough.Well thought,well written, Bill Gates!

text: On my first day as CEO at the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, I'm reflecting on the huge responsibility that comes with this job. Knowing that we at the foundation can't achieve our ambitious goals alone, I am deeply committed to building our community of partners and to ensuring that our culture at the foundation enables the impact we aspire to have. Building a community is easy to say, but I know that it will take a lot of work. So I've set myself a goal to take the extra time to connect with colleagues and partners, to listen to their experiences and ask them, and myself, the tough questions. I am especially excited to spend my first week as CEO in South Africa, where I was raised and where exposure to apartheid first instilled in me a desire to use the advantages afforded to me to help tackle injustice and inequality. I can't think of a better place to start this next chapter in my career (and I'm pretty happy that it gives me a chance to see my parents as well).
